docs.title: Welcome to the Cloud-Ops-Wiki
docs.text:
Your central hub for Platform Engineering, Cloud Infrastructure, and SRE Documentation.

Welcome aboard! This wiki is maintained by the Platform Engineering team. Whether you are a new hire getting your workstation configured, or an on-call engineer fighting a 3 AM fire, you'll find what you need here.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• 🚀 Getting Started: Setup your local dev environment, get IAM access, and spin up a local testing cluster.
  • 🚨 Troubleshooting: Incident response playbooks, runbooks, and how to query logs/metrics.
  • 📐 Standards & Architecture: Naming conventions, system architecture diagrams, and Terraform best practices.
  • 🛠️ Tools: Links to internal CI/CD pipelines, dashboards, logs, and external vendors.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
This documentation is treated as code. If you see something out of date, please submit a Pull Request!

git clone git@github.com:company/cloud-ops-wiki.git
cd cloud-ops-wiki
# Edit files in /docs
# Remember that CI will reject PRs with invalid markdown formatting!

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to the Cloud Operations & Platform Engineering team! This guide will help you set up your local environment and get productive right away.

docs.title: Day 1 Checklist
docs.text:
• Request Identity and Access Management (IAM) Roles via IT Portal.
  • Connect to the corporate VPN.
  • Join the #platform-eng-alerts and #cloud-ops-general Slack channels.
  • Install your preferred IDE or Cloud Editor.

docs.title: Development Environment Setup
docs.text:
Ensure you have the following tools installed locally to interact with our infrastructure:

macOSLinuxWindows 
brew install kubectl
brew install k9s
brew install terraform
brew install awscli

# Install curl, unzip, and other dependencies before proceeding
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y curl unzip
# Follow official guides to install kubectl, terraform, awscli...

winget install Kubernetes.kubectl
winget install Hashicorp.Terraform
winget install Amazon.AWSCLI

docs.title: Local Sandbox
docs.text:
We use k3d for local Kubernetes testing. Run the following to spin up a quick development cluster:

k3d cluster create ops-sandbox --servers 1 --agents 2

[!TIP] Make sure Docker is running on your machine before attempting to spin up the local cluster.

docs.title: Core Repositories
docs.text: Repository Purpose Ownership cloud-ops-infra Main Terraform configuration for AWS / GCP Platform Team k8s-manifests Flux/ArgoCD GitOps cluster state Platform Team internal-tools-cli Go-based utility wrappers for internal APIs Developer Experience

docs.title: Architecture & Standards
docs.text: This section contains our standard operating procedures, architectural conventions, and naming standards to ensure consistency across the platform.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Below is a sample architecture representing how incoming traffic routes to our microservices via our standard API Gateway and Kubernetes Ingress.

graph TD
    Client(("Client")) --> Cloudflare["Cloudflare CDN / WAF"]
    Cloudflare --> ALB["AWS Application Load Balancer"]

    subgraph k8s_cluster ["Kubernetes Cluster"]
        NginxIngress["Ingress Controller (NGINX)"]
        Svc1["Frontend Service"]
        Svc2["API Gateway Service"]
        Pod1("Frontend Pods")
        Pod2("Auth Service")
        Pod3("Billing Service")

        NginxIngress --> Svc1
        NginxIngress --> Svc2
        Svc1 --> Pod1
        Svc2 --> Pod2
        Svc2 --> Pod3
    end

    ALB --> NginxIngress

    Pod2 --> Redis[("Redis Cache")]
    Pod3 --> DB[("PostgreSQL")]

    classDef aws_style fill:#FF9900,stroke:#232F3E,stroke-width:2px,color:white;
    classDef k8s_style fill:#326CE5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:white;
    classDef db_style fill:#336791,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:white;

    class ALB aws_style;
    class NginxIngress,Svc1,Svc2 k8s_style;
    class DB db_style;

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Consistency makes automation easier. Please adhere to the following when creating new resources:

docs.title: AWS Resources
docs.text:
Use the format: <environment>-<region>-<service>-<name>

  • Example (S3): prod-euwest1-s3-applogs
  • Example (ALB): staging-useast1-alb-api

docs.title: Kubernetes Namespaces
docs.text:
Namespaces should reflect the team or the primary generic function.

  • monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana)
  • ingress-nginx (Ingress controllers)
  • team-billing
  • team-auth

docs.title: GitOps Workflow
docs.text:
We practice GitOps. Direct cluster mutation via kubectl apply is disabled in staging and prod.

  1. Make a branch modifying the k8s-manifests repo.
  2. Open a Pull Request.
  3. CI runs kubeval and conftest policies.
  4. Once approved and merged to main, ArgoCD will automatically sync the changes.

docs.title: Terraform Best Practices
docs.text:
• Never use local state. Always use the configured S3 backend with DynamoDB locking.
  • Pin module versions in the main.tf files.
  • Always run terraform plan and attach the output to the PR for review.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: When the pager goes off at 3 AM, refer to this directory. The information here is designed to be scannable, factual, and action-oriented.

docs.title: Incident Response Playbook
docs.text: [!CAUTION] If a system is completely offline and impacting external customers, immediately open a Sev1 Incident via PagerDuty and escalate to the Incident Commander.

docs.title: Escalation Matrix
docs.text:
1. Level 1 (On-Call): Acknowledges the alert, begins investigation, and posts findings to the incident slack channel.
  2. Level 2 (SME): Brought in if the issue cannot be localized within 15 minutes.
  3. Level 3 (Leadership): Briefed if the outage duration is expected to exceed the SLA.

docs.title: Observability Hub
docs.text:
Where to look when something breaks:

  • Logs: Elastic/Kibana Dashboard
  • Metrics: Grafana Main UI
  • Traces: Datadog APM

docs.title: High CPU Usage on Node Pool
docs.text:
1. Identify the cluster and node pool via Grafana.
  2. Run kubectl top nodes and kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu
  3. If a specific deployment is consuming resources, check if a rogue loop is present in logs or if horizontal scaling limits (HPA) need to be adjusted.

docs.title: Database Connection Pool Exhausted
docs.text:
1. Validate current connections in the AWS RDS dashboard.
  2. Verify if an application recently deployed is leaking connections or not using connection pooling (e.g. PgBouncer).
  3. Temporary mitigation: Terminate idle sessions if absolute emergency, but prioritize rolling back the suspected offending deployment.
